text: This is a sixty-five-year-old male with an incarcerated left inguinal scrotal hernia. He was sent to me by the gastroenterologist because his CAT scan showed a large incarcerated inguinal scrotal hernia containing his sigmoid colon. They weren't able to do his CAT scan, so they needed him to have this repaired prior.

The surgeon begins the medial dissection by accessing the preperitoneal pocket, carefully pulling down the flap to enhance visualization of the colon and surrounding structures. The flap is lifted to check the condition of the colon, ensuring adequate exposure on both sides of the inguinal scrotal hernia defect.
The surgeon examines a thickened hernia sac that is fused to the ilio-pubic tract below, noting its substantial thickness compared to typical hernia sacs.
The hernia sac is prominently visible as the surgeon sweeps surrounding tissues to locate the vas deferens. The colon is positioned lower in the surgical field.

The surgeon navigates around the corner, encountering a significant amount of fat on the iliacs. The hernia sac is lifted at multiple points, revealing filmy attachments. The mesh is positioned perfectly, with high lateral fixation above the myopectenial orifice, and medial fixation near the main coronavorous vein, allowing the mesh to curl around the triangle of doom to prevent recurrence. A supermedial fixation is applied for standard closure.
